history.label: Origins
history.text: Jackson was originally populated by Native American tribes including the Shoshoni, Crow, Blackfeet, Bannock, and Gros Ventre.
Of the 2,697,000 acres (1,091,000 ha) in Teton County, 97% are federally or state owned/managed.

history.label: 1800s
history.text: In the early 1800s, the locality became a prime area for trappers and mountain men to travel through, one example being John Colter.
After being discharged from the Corps of Discovery of the Lewis and Clark Expedition in 1806 at Fort Mandan, in present-day North Dakota, Colter visited Jackson Hole during the winter of 1807/1808.
As part of the Hayden Expedition of 1871 and 1872, William Henry Jackson took the first photographs of the Teton Range and Yellowstone.
Yellowstone National Park became the first National Park in 1872.
The town of Jackson was named in 1894 and incorporated in 1914.
The town of Jackson elected an all-woman city government (including town council and mayor, who in turn appointed women to town marshal, town clerk and treasurer) in 1920.
Grand Teton National Park was created in 1929 and greatly expanded in 1950 after John D.
The first ski rope tow was built at Teton Pass in 1937 and Snow King Resort was established in 1930.
In 2009, the town of Jackson was designated as a Preserve America Community.

history.label: Today
history.text: Other mountain men who visited the valley include Jim Bridger, Jedediah Smith, and William Sublette, who are responsible for many of the names in the area.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: His photographs along with the sketches by Tom Moran, were important evidence to convince Congress to protect Yellowstone National Park.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The town of Jackson is the county seat of Teton County and the only incorporated municipality in the region.
